question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Redox reactions only have applications in industrial settings.
The concept of the Hydrogen Economy, which involves using hydrogen as a fuel, is related to redox reactions.
explanation: Redox reactions are not limited to industrial applications; they also occur in biological, pharmaceutical, and environmental contexts. The concept of hydrogen economy involves redox processes, particularly in the context of fuel cells where hydrogen is oxidized. Correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Redox reactions are utilized in the extraction of highly reactive metals.
Redox reactions are also important in the manufacturing of chemical compounds such as caustic soda.
explanation: Redox reactions are fundamental in extracting highly reactive metals and manufacturing chemical compounds like caustic soda, as they involve the transfer of electrons between substances. Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question: 100 mL of ozone at STP were passed through 100 mL of 10 volume ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}}$ solution. What is the volume strength of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}}$ after the reaction?
explanation:
$\begin{array}{l} \quad \quad {{\rm{O}}_3} \to \,\,\,\,\,\,\,{{\rm{O}}_2} +\,\,\,\, {\rm{O}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(1)}}\\ \quad \quad {{\rm{H}}_2}{{\rm{O}}_2} \to {{\rm{H}}_2}{\rm{O}} + {\rm{O}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)\\ \,\,{\rm{O}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, + \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{O}}\,\,\, \to \,\,\,\,{{\rm{O}}_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)\\ 1/2{\rm{vol}}\,\,\,\quad 1/2\,\,\,{\rm{vol}}\quad \,\,\,1{\rm{vol}} \end{array}$
From Eqs. (1) and (2),
we infer that ${\mathrm{100 \mathrm{~mL}_{\mathrm{m}}}}$ of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{3}}}$ at STP will produce 100 mL of molecular ${\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}$ as such and 100 mL of oxygen molecule after reaction with ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}}$.
This new volume of 100 mL of molecular oxygen after reaction with ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}}$ is contributed equally by ${\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{3}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}}$. Thus, 50 mL of oxygen have been contributed by ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}}$.
Again, we know
Volume of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \times}}$ Volume strength of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}}$
$=\text { Volume of } \mathrm{O}_{2} \text { at STP }$
${\mathrm{\therefore 100 \mathrm{~mL}}}$ of ${\rm{'10}}\,{\rm{V}}\,{\rm{'}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} \equiv {\rm{1000}}\,{\rm{mL}}$ of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}$ at STP After utilisation of 50 mL of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}$, according to Eq. (iii), the balance ${\mathrm{(1000-50)=950 \mathrm{~mL}}}$ of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}$ at STP are still retainable by 100 mL of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}}$.
Hence volume strength of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}}$ after reaction$=\dfrac{\text { Volume of } \mathrm{O}_{2} \text { at STP }}{\text { Volume of } \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}=\dfrac{950}{100}=9.5 \mathrm{~V}$
${\mathrm{\therefore}}$ Volume strength ${\mathrm{=9.5}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
For the redox reaction,
$\mathrm{xMnO}_{4}^{-}+\mathrm{yH}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}+\mathrm{zH}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{mMn}^{2+}$
$+\mathrm{nCO}_{2}+\mathrm{pH}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

The balanced equation is given as

question:

$\mathrm{aKNO}_{3}+5 \mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11} \rightarrow$ $\mathrm{bN}_{2}+\mathrm{cCO}_{2}+\mathrm{dH}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{eK}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$
explanation:
$\left. {2\mathop {{\text{KN}}}\limits^{( + 5)} {{\text{O}}_3}\xrightarrow{{10\,{{\text{e}}^ - }}}\mathop {{{\text{N}}_2}}\limits^{(0)} } \right] \times 24$
$\left. {{{\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^{(0)} }_{12}}{{\text{H}}_{22}}{{\text{O}}_{11}}\xrightarrow{{48\,{{\text{e}}^ - }}}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^{( + 4)} } \right] \times 5$
$48 \mathrm{KNO}_{3}+5 \mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11} \rightarrow$
$24 \mathrm{~N}_{2}+55 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+36 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+24 \mathrm{~K}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$

question: $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ oxidises oxalic acid in acidic medium. The number of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ molecules produced per mole of $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ is
explanation:

question:
The coefficients of $\mathrm{I}^{-}, \mathrm{IO}_{3}^{-}$and $\mathrm{H}^{+}$in the redox reaction,
$\mathrm{I}^{-}+\mathrm{IO}_{3}^{-}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
in the balanced form respectively are
explanation:
${{\rm{I}}^ - }{\rm{ + }}\left( {\mathop {\rm{I}}\limits^{{\rm{ + 5}}} {\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}^ - } \right){\rm{ + }}{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}} \to \mathop {{{\rm{I}}_{\rm{2}}}}\limits^{\rm{0}} {\rm{ + }}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
Oxidation half reaction:
$2{{\rm{I}}^ - } \to {{\rm{I}}_2} + 2{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{ - }}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Reduction half reaction
$10{{\rm{e}}^ - } + 2(\mathop {\rm{I}}\limits^{ + 5} {\rm{O}}_3^ - ) \to \mathop {{{\rm{I}}_2}}\limits^0 {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (2)$
In order to cancel the electrons, multiplying Eq. (1) by 5 and then adding to Eq. (2), we get
$10 \mathrm{I}^{-}+2 \mathrm{IO}_{3}^{-} \rightarrow 6 \mathrm{I}_{2}$
To balance $\mathrm{O}$-atom, add $6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ molecules on RHS and $12 \mathrm{H}^{+}$on LHS, then
$10 \mathrm{I}^{-}+2 \mathrm{IO}_{3}^{-}+12 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow 6 \mathrm{I}_{2}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
or $5 \mathrm{I}^{-}+\mathrm{IO}_{3}^{-}+6 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{I}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Methods to Balance Redox Reactions: Oxidation Number Method and Ion Electron Method

question:

${ }_{-} \mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+{ }_{-} \mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}_{4} \mathrm{C}+$ _CO

explanation:
$2 \mathrm{~B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+7 \mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}_{4} \mathrm{C}+6 \mathrm{CO}$
Total sum of coefficients $=2+7+1+6=16$.

question:

explanation:

$\left.2 \mathrm{BrF}_{3} \xrightarrow{-6} \mathrm{Br}_{2}\right) 20$
$12 \mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}+40 \mathrm{BrF}_{3} \rightarrow 24 \mathrm{IF}_{5}+30 \,\,\mathrm{O}_{2}+20 \mathrm{Br}_{2}$

question:
When the equation for the reaction of $\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})$ and $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$ is balanced correctly,
$\_{\rm{S}}{{\rm{n}}^{{\rm{ + 2}}}}({\rm{aq}}) + \_{\rm{MnO}}_4^ - ({\rm{aq}}) + \_{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}({\rm{aq}}) \to $
$\_{\rm{S}}{{\rm{n}}^{{\rm{4 + }}}}({\rm{aq}}) + \_{\rm{M}}{{\rm{n}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}({\rm{aq}}) + \_{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}({\rm{l}})$
what is the ratio of $\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$?
explanation:
$5\,\,({\text{S}}{{\text{n}}^{ + 2}}\xrightarrow{{ + 2}}{\text{S}}{{\text{n}}^{ + 4}}$
$2\,({\text{MnO}}_4^ - \xrightarrow{{ - 5}}{\text{M}}{{\text{n}}^{ + 2}}$
and the balanced equation is
$\begin{aligned}& 5 \mathrm{Sn}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+16 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \\& 5 \mathrm{Sn}^{4+}+2 \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$
so, the ratio is $5 / 2$

question:
The values of ${\rm{x}},{\rm{y}}$ and ${\rm{z}}$ in the reaction are respectively
$4 \mathrm{KO}_{2}+\mathrm{xH}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{yCO}_{2} \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{KHCO}_{3}+\mathrm{zO}_{2}$
explanation:
$4 \mathrm{KO}_{2}+\mathrm{xH}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{yCO}_{2} \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{KHCO}_{3}+\mathrm{zO}_{2}$
Equating H on both sides: $2 \mathrm{x}=4 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=2$
Equating C on both sides: $\mathrm{y}=4$
Equating O on both sides: $8+\mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{y}$
$ = (4 \times 3) + 2{\rm{z}}$
$\Rightarrow 8+2+(2 \times 4)=12+2 \mathrm{z}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{z}=3$
$4 \mathrm{KO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+4 \mathrm{CO}_{2} \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{KHCO}_{3}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Methods to Balance Redox Reactions: Oxidation Number Method and Ion Electron Method

question:
In the equation
$\rm{\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}+\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O},}$
the coefficients of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}$ and $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}^{+}}$are respectively
explanation:
$\mathrm{\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}+\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$
Oxidation half equation
${\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}} \to {\rm{F}}{{\rm{e}}^{{\rm{3 + }}}}{\rm{ + }}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{ - }}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Reduction half equation
$\mathop {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}}\limits^{{\rm{ + 6}}} {\rm{O}}_{\rm{7}}^{{\rm{2 - }}} \to {\rm{2C}}{{\rm{r}}^{{\rm{3 + }}}}{\rm{ + 6}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{ - }}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
(1) $ \times {\rm{6}} \Rightarrow {\rm{6F}}{{\rm{e}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}} \to {\rm{6F}}{{\rm{e}}^{{\rm{3 + }}}}{\rm{ + 6}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{ - }}}({\rm{2}}){\rm{ + }}({\rm{3}})\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
Balancing $\mathrm{\mathrm{O}}$ by adding sufficient $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$ molecules to the deficient side,
$\mathrm{\Rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-} \rightarrow 6 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}}$
Balancing $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}}$ by adding sufficient $\mathrm{H^{+}}$ ions to the deficient side,
$\rm{6 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow 6 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$

question: The number of moles of electron required to reduce 0.2 mole of $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}$ to $\mathrm{Cr}^{+3}$ is
explanation:
$\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}}\limits^{ + 6} {\text{O}}_7^{2 - } + 6{{\text{e}}^ - } \to 2{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}^{3 + }}$
Change in oxidation state $=+3$ per chromium atom.
1 mole of $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}$ required $=6 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
$\therefore 0.2$ moles of Cr required $=0.2 \times 6$
$=1.2 \text { moles of electrons }$

question: What is the equivalent weight of methanol, if one mole of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$ is combusted to form CO and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ ?
explanation:
Combustion of one mole of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$ :
$\stackrel{(-2)}{\mathrm{CH}_{3}} \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \stackrel{(+2)}{\mathrm{CO}}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
Difference in oxidation number $=4$
$\therefore$ Equivalent weight of methanol
$=\dfrac{\text { molecular weight of } \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}}{\text { change in oxidation number }}=\dfrac{32}{4}=8$

question: The molar mass of a metallic oxide $\mathrm{\mathrm{MO}}$ is 40 . The equivalent mass of the element will be

question: In the reaction $\mathrm{\mathrm{Al}+\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+\mathrm{Fe}}$ what is the total no. of electrons transferred during the change?
explanation:
${\rm{2A}}{{\rm{l}}^{\rm{0}}} \to {\rm{Al}}_{\rm{2}}^{{\rm{ + 3}}}{\rm{ + 6}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{ - }}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
${\rm{8e + Fe}}_{\rm{3}}^{{\rm{ + 8/3}}} \to {\rm{3F}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{0}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Multiplying Eq. (1) by 4 and Eq. (2) by 3, then on addition
${\rm{8A}}{{\rm{l}}^{\rm{0}}} \to {\rm{4Al}}_{\rm{2}}^{{\rm{ + 3}}}{\rm{ + 24e}}$
${\rm{24e + 3Fe}}_{\rm{3}}^{{\rm{ + 8/3}}} \to {\rm{9F}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{0}}}$
$\overline {{\rm{8A}}{{\rm{l}}^{\rm{0}}}{\rm{ + 3F}}{{\rm{e}}^{{\rm{ + 8/3}}}} \to {\rm{9F}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{0}}}{\rm{ + 4Al}}_{\rm{2}}^{{\rm{ + 3}}}} $
$\mathrm{8 \mathrm{Al}+3 \mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+9 \mathrm{Fe}}$
Therefore, it is clear that total no. of electrons transferred during change $\mathrm{=24}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Match the following columns based on the concepts related to redox reactions and electrode processes, and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
(A - R) The reaction ${\text{Zn}}({\text{s}})$ with ${\text{C}}{{\text{u}}^{2 + }}({\text{aq}})$ leading to $({\text{Z}}{{\text{n}}^{2 + }})\,({\text{aq}})$ and ${\text{Cu}}\,({\text{s}})$ is a classic example of a displacement reaction where zinc displaces copper.
(B - S) The equation for the reduction of permanganate ion $\left( {{\text{MnO}}_4^ - } \right)$ to manganese $({\text{M}}{{\text{n}}^{2 + }})$ under acidic conditions shows the calculation for the standard electrode potential for this reaction.
(C - P) The electrochemical series lists elements based on their tendency to lose or gain electrons, reflecting their relative strengths as oxidizing or reducing agents.
(D - Q) The Daniell cell setup involves a salt bridge, which is essential for completing the electrical circuit between the zinc and copper solutions, allowing for ion flow and preventing the solutions from mixing directly.
Correct option is (2).

question: In which of the following compounds, an element exhibits two different oxidation states?
explanation:
Oxidation states of “N” in $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ are $3,+5$.$\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ contains $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$and $\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$ions.
In $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+} ; \mathrm{x}+4(+1)=+1$
$x=-3$
In $\mathrm{NO}_{3}{ }^{-} ; \mathrm{x}+3(-2)=-1$
$x=+5$
subject: Chemistry

question: The oxidation number of nitrogen atoms in $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ are
explanation:
$\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ is formed by $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$and $\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$.
Let oxidation number of N is x .
In $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$
$\begin{aligned}& x+4=+1 \\& x=-3\end{aligned}$
In $\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$
$\begin{aligned}& x+3(-2)=-1 \\& x=+5\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question: In $\mathrm{Pb}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$, the different oxidation states of Pb is/are
explanation:

${\text{Pb}}{{\text{O}}_2} \to {\text{x}} + 2 \times ( - 2) = 0 \Rightarrow {\text{x}} - 4 = 0$ $ \Rightarrow {\text{x}} = + \,4$

question: The oxidation state of Cr in $\mathrm{CrO}_{5}$ is
explanation:

Let, the oxidation number of Cr is x .
Then, $\mathrm{x}+4(-1)+1(-2)=0$
$\mathrm{x}=+6$

question: The oxidation states of three carbon atoms in carbon suboxide $\left(\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{2}\right)$ respectively are
explanation:
The structure of $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ is
$\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}$
Assigning the oxidation number to each C atom in molecule considering oxidation number of oxygen as $ - 2$
We get, oxidation state as $\mathrm{O}=\stackrel{+2}{\mathrm{C}}=\stackrel{0}{\mathrm{C}}=\stackrel{+2}{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{O}$
Thus, two C - atoms linked with O - atom has $ + 2$ oxidation state and carbon atom in the centre has 0 oxidation state.

$2+\mathrm{x}-8=0$
$\mathrm{x}=+6$.

question: Which of the following cannot act as an oxidising agent?
explanation:
An oxidizing agent must undergo reduction. Here, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}^{3-}}}$ is already in its lowest oxidation state of $-$3 and cannot be reduced further. Thus, it cannot be used as an oxidizing agent and it acts as only reducing agent.
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Mn}^{7+}, \mathrm{S}^{6+}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Br}^{5+}}}$ can be reduced to lower states and behave as oxidising agents. So, the correct option is (3).

$\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$has a -1 charge.

The oxidation number of $\mathrm{P}$ in $\mathrm{Mg}_{2} \mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ can be calculated as follows: Suppose the oxidation number of $\mathrm{P}$ be $\mathrm{x}$
then, $2(+2)+2 \mathrm{x}+(-14)=0$
$4 + 2{\rm{x}} - 14 = 0$

${\rm{x}} = + 5$

$2{\rm{x}} = 12 \Rightarrow {\rm{x}} = 6$

question:
Assertion: Stannous chloride gives grey precipitate with mercuric chloride, but stannic chloride does not do so.


Reason: Stannous chloride is a powerful oxidising agent which oxidises mercuric chloride to metallic mercury.
explanation:
$\mathrm{SnCl}_{2}$ (stannous chloride) on reaction with mercuric chloride $\left(\mathrm{HgCl}_{2}\right)$, oxidises to $\mathrm{SnCl}_{4}$ (stannic chloride)
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{SnCl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{HgCl}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{SnCl}_{4}+\mathrm{Hg}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \\& \mathrm{SnCl}_{2}+\mathrm{Hg}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \rightarrow \underset{\text { (Greyish) }}{2 \mathrm{Hg}}+\mathrm{SnCl}_{4}\end{aligned}$
So in this reaction, stannous chloride is acting as reducing agent that reduces $\mathrm{Hg}$ (II) to $\mathrm{Hg}$ (I) and then to $\mathrm{Hg}(0)$ So the option (3) is correct

So the option (1) is correct.

explanation: The oxidation state of Oxygen in $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ is zero, while the oxidation state of $\mathrm{S}$ in $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ is ${\text{ + 4}}$ . Sulfur dioxide can function as both oxidising and reducing agent whereas ozone can only act as an oxidizing agent due to oxygen's oxidation state which decreases but not increases in reactions. So, the option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{PbCl}_{4}$ is a powerful oxidising agent.


Reason: $\mathrm{PbCl}_{4}$ is more stable than $\mathrm{PbCl}_{2}$.
explanation: $\mathrm{Pb}$ loses two p-electrons to form stable $\mathrm{PbCl}_{2}$. It has to loose its s electrons as well to form $\mathrm{PbCl}_{4}$, which is less stable. This is due to inert pair effect (reluctance of ns elecrons to take part in the reaction). Thus, due to less stability, $\mathrm{PbCl}_{4}$ easily gains $2 \mathrm{e}$ - to became more stable $\mathrm{PbCl}_{2}$. And in turn behaves as a strong oxidising agent. So the option (3) is correct.

Reason: This is due to the increasing stability of the lower species to which they are reduced.
explanation: Potassium permanganate $\left(\mathrm{KMNO}_{4}\right)$ is a stronger and more versatile oxidizing agent than potassium dichromate $\left(\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}\right)$ due to factors including the higher oxidation state of manganese $(+7)$, its effectiveness under both acidic and basic conditions, larger atom size, greater stability of oxidation states, and higher redox potential. So the option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$is reduced to $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ in acidic medium.


Reason: In acidic medium, following reaction takes place. $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$
explanation:
The reduction of $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$ions can occur differently depending on the $\mathrm{pH}$ of the medium. In a mild basic or neutral medium:
$\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-2}$
In an acidic medium:
$\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+5 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{+2}$
In a strong basic medium:
$\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{+4}$
Thus, the option (3) is correct.

Reason: $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ (formaldehyde) is a covalent compound.
explanation:
Oxidation numbers are determined by specific rules. Hydrogen $(\mathrm{H})$ is assigned an oxidation state of $(+ 1)$ , and oxygen $(\mathrm{O})$ of $(- 2)$ . Let the oxidation number of carbon $(\mathrm{C})$ in $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ be $\mathrm{x}$. Then
$\mathrm{x}+2(+1)+(-2)=0$
$\mathrm{x}=0$
So, in this compound, carbon $(\mathrm{C})$ has an oxidation number of $0 . \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is a covalent compound. So the option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

$\begin{gathered}+1+\mathrm{x}+3(-2)=-1 \\\therefore \mathrm{x}=+4\end{gathered}$
Sulphur can show variable O.N. from $(- 2)$ to $(+ 6)$ in different compounds So the option (2) is correct.

${\text{2}}{\mathop {\text{S}}\limits^{{\text{ + 2}}} _{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}^{\mathop {{\text{2 - }}}\limits^{{\text{ + 2}}} }{\text{(aq) + I}}_{\text{2}}^{\text{0}}{\text{(s)}} \to \mathop {{\text{ }}{{\text{S}}_{\text{4}}}}\limits^{{\text{2}}{\text{.5}}} {\text{O}}_{\text{6}}^{{\text{2 - }}}{\text{(aq) + 2}}{{\text{I}}^{\text{ - }}}{\text{(aq)}}$

Bromine being stronger oxidising agent than $\mathrm{\mathrm{I}_{2}}$, oxidises $\mathrm{\mathrm{S}}$ of $\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}}$ to $\mathrm{\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}}$ whereas $\mathrm{\mathrm{I}_{2}}$ oxidises it only into $\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{6}^{2-}}$ ion.

question: In which of the following compounds, nitrogen exhibits the highest oxidation state?
explanation:
Let the oxidation state of nitrogen in the given compounds be $\mathrm{\mathrm{x}}$.

$ \Rightarrow \,\,x = - \frac{1}{3}$

$\rm{x+2-2+1=0 \Rightarrow x=-1}$
(c) $\mathrm{\stackrel{x+1}{N_{2}} H_{4} \Rightarrow 2(x)+(+1) 4=0}$
$\rm{\Rightarrow 2 x=-4 \Rightarrow x=-2}$
(d) $\mathrm{\stackrel{x+1}{N H_{3}} \Rightarrow x+(+1) 3=0}$$\rm{x=-3}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ Oxidation state of nitrogen is highest in $\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{3} \mathrm{H}}$.

question: In which substance does nitrogen exhibit the lowest oxidation state?
explanation:
Oxidation state of nitrogen gas $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{N}_{2}\right)}$ is zero, as both the nitrogen atom possess same electronegativity as well as same number of electrons. The oxidation states of ammonia, nitrous oxide and nitric oxide are given below.
Ammonia $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)}$
${\rm{x + 3}}({\rm{1}}){\rm{ = 0}}$
${\rm{x = - 3}}$
Nitrous oxide $\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}$
${\rm{2}}({\rm{x}}){\rm{ + 1}}({\rm{ - 2}}){\rm{ = 0}}$
${\rm{2x = 2}}$
${\rm{x = + 1}}$
Nitric oxide (NO)
${\rm{x + }}({\rm{ - 2}}){\rm{ = 0}}$
${\rm{x = + 2}}$
Thus, nitrogen exhibits lowest oxidation state in case of ammonia.
subject: Chemistry

question: The highest oxidation state in plutonium (At. no. $\mathrm{=94)}$ is
explanation: The highest oxidation state in plutonium (Atomic number $\mathrm{=94}$ ) is +7 . Plutonium $\mathrm{(\mathrm{Pu})}$ can show oxidation state of $\mathrm{+3,+4,+5,+6}$ and +7 in its compound.

${\rm{2 + 2x - 14 = 0}}$
${\rm{2x = 12x = + 6}}$

question:

$\mathrm{\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}+\mathrm{KClO}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{KCl}+\mathrm{Fe}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}}$
I. $\mathrm{\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}}$ acts as reducing agent.
II. $\mathrm{\mathrm{KClO}_{3}}$ is reduced.
III. The change in oxidation number of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}}$ is 2.
IV. The change in oxidation number of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}}$ is 6 .
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
explanation:
Statement I, II and IV are correct, while the statement III is incorrect.
Its correct form is as follows:
$\rm{\stackrel{+2}{\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}}+\mathrm{K} \mathrm{ClO}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{+5} \stackrel{-1}{\mathrm{C}}+\mathrm{Fe}_{2}^{+3}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}^{-2}}$
The change in oxidation state of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}=3-2=1}$.

question: Oxidation number of cobalt in $\mathrm{\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \mathrm{Br}}$ is
explanation:
Let the O.N. of Co be $\mathrm{x}$
O.N. of $\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}$ is zero
O.N. of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}}$ is ${\rm{ - 1}}$
O.N. of $\mathrm{\mathrm{Br}}$ is ${\rm{ - 1}}$
Hence, $\mathrm{x+6(0)-1 \times 2-1=0}$
$\mathrm{\therefore \quad x=+3}$
so, the oxidation number of cobalt in the given complex compound is +3 .

or $\mathrm{\mathrm{KI}_{3}}$ is $\mathrm{\mathrm{KI}+\mathrm{I}_{2}}$
I has two oxidation no. ${\rm{ - 1}}$ and 0 respectively.
However factually speaking oxidation number of
$\mathrm{\mathrm{I}}$ in $\mathrm{\mathrm{KI}_{3}}$ is on average of two values -1 and 0 .
Average O.N. $\mathrm{=\dfrac{-1+2 \times(0)}{3}=-\dfrac{1}{3}}$

question: Which of the following reactions is neither oxidation nor reduction?
explanation:
In the reaction
$\mathrm{\mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}}$
The O.N. of $\mathrm{\mathrm{P}}$ in $\mathrm{P_{2} O_{5}}$ is
$\mathrm{2 x+5(-2)=0}$ or $\mathrm{x=+5}$
The O.N. of $\mathrm{\mathrm{P}}$ in $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}}$ is
$\mathrm{4(+1)+2(x)+7(-2)=0}$
$\mathrm{2 x=10}$ or $\mathrm{x=+5}$
Since there is no change in O.N. of $\mathrm{P}$, hence the above reaction is neither oxidation nor reduction.

question: Match the following columns based on the electron transfer concept of redox reactions, and choose the correct options from the codes given below.

(B - R) The reaction ${\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_2} + 2{{\text{e}}^ - } \to 2{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ - }$ involves the gain of electrons by chlorine, hence it is a reduction half-reaction, demonstrating the gain of electrons.
(C - Q) The electron donor acts as a reducing agent because it donates electrons to another species.
(D - P) The electron acceptor acts as an oxidising agent because it accepts electrons from another species, facilitating oxidation. Correct option is (2).

III. Adition of electronegative element.

Reduction is the process which involves removal of oxygen or electronegative element from a substance or addition of hydrogen or electropositive element to a substance.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The electrode potential of the zinc electrode in the Daniell cell is more negative than that of the copper electrode.
A more negative electrode potential indicates a greater tendency to lose electrons.
explanation: Statement  A  is true because in a Daniell cell, zinc acts as the anode and has a more negative electrode potential compared to copper, which acts as the cathode. Statement B is also true as a more negative electrode potential does indeed indicate a greater tendency of the substance to lose electrons, thus acting as an anode. Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Electrode potential is a measure of the tendency of a substance to gain or lose electrons.
The standard electrode potential is determined at a temperature of $25^\circ {\text{C}}$ with all solutes at 1 M concentration.
explanation: Statement  A  is true as electrode potential indeed measures the tendency of a substance to be oxidized or reduced (gain or lose electrons). Statement B is also true because standard electrode potentials are determined under standard conditions, which include a temperature of  $25^\circ {\text{C}}\,(298\,{\text{K}})$  and concentration of 1 M.   Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Assertion: Salts like $\mathrm{KCl}, \mathrm{KNO}_{3}$ i.e., inert electrolytes are used in salt bridge.


Reason: An inert electrolyte can easily be filled in the U-tube.
explanation: Inert electrolytes, such as salts like potassium chloride $(\mathrm{KCl})$ or sodium sulfate $\left(\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)$, are used in salt bridge because they enhance electrical conductivity, maintain charge neutrality, minimize interference in redox reactions, and provide a stable background for monitoring electrochemical changes without actively participating in chemical transformations. So the option (3) is correct.

explanation: As we know $\mathrm{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}_{2}}$ couple has zero standard reduction potential so, ions having positive $\mathrm{\mathrm{E}^{\circ}}$ value are weaker reducing agent, while ions having negative $\mathrm{\mathrm{E}^{\circ}}$ value are stronger reducing agent.

question:
Consider the following redox couples:
I. $\mathrm{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}}=-0.76}$.
II. $\mathrm{\mathrm{Ag}^{2+} / \mathrm{Ag}, \mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}}=+0.80 \mathrm{~V}}$.
III. $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{E}^{o}=+0.34 \mathrm{v}}$.
IV. $\mathrm{\mathrm{Hg}^{2+} / \mathrm{Hg} ; \mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}}=+0.885}$
Which of the above will act as anode when connected to standard hydrogen electrode which has $\mathrm{\mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}}}$ value given as zero?
explanation:
When the value of standard reduction potential is negative, the electrode undergoes oxidation and acts as anode. Thus, $\mathrm{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}$; $\mathrm{\mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}}=-0.76}$ will acts as a anode, when connected to standard hydrogen electrode.
Thus, statement I is correct.

question:
Iron(III) catalyses the reaction between iodide and persulphate ions, in which
(I) ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}}}$ oxidises the iodide ion
(II) ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}}}$ oxidises the persulphate ion
(III) ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}}$ reduces the iodide ion
(IV) ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}}$ reduces the persulphate ion
Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
explanation:
Mechanism of the reaction is
${\mathrm{2 \mathrm{I}^{-}+2 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{2}+2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}}$
${\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{Fe}^{+3}\right.}}$ oxidises ${\mathrm{\mathrm{I}^{-}}}$to ${\mathrm{\left.\mathrm{I}_{2}\right)}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8}^{2-}+2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}}$ reduces ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8}^{2-}}}$ into ${\mathrm{\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}}}$.
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Chemistry

question: The ratio of $\mathrm{FeC}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ (ferrous oxalate) and ferric oxalate used per mole of $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}$ in acidic medium is ____.
explanation:
$\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right)$ is oxidised to $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}$ to $2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$
$\left. \begin{gathered} {\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{2 + }}}} \to {\text{F}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{3 + }}}} + {{\text{e}}^ - } \hfill \\ {\text{CrO}}_4^{2 - } \to 2{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_2} + 2{{\text{e}}^ - } \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{\text{n}} = 3$
$\left[ {6{{\text{e}}^ - } + {\text{C}}{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}_7^{2 - } \to 2{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}^{{\text{3 + }}}}({\text{n}} = 6)} \right]$
In $\mathrm{Fe}_{2}\left(\mathrm{CrO}_{4}\right)_{3}, \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ is not oxidised, but $3 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{-}$is oxidised to $6 \mathrm{CO}_{2}$. $({\text{So}}\,\,\,\,{\text{n}} = 6)$
${\text{Fe}}\left( {{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}} \right) \equiv {\text{C}}{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}_7^{2 - }\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {\text{Fe}}{\left( {{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}} \right)_3} \equiv {\text{C}}{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}_7^{2 - }$
$1{\text{Eq}} \equiv 1{\text{Eq}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,1{\text{Eq}} \equiv 1{\text{Eq}}$
$\frac{1}{3}\,{\text{mol}} \equiv \frac{1}{6}\,{\text{mol}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{1}{3}\,{\text{mol}} \equiv \frac{1}{6}\,{\text{mol}}$
$1\,{\text{mol}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}_7^{2 - }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,1\,{\text{mol}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}_7^{2 - }$
$ = \frac{6}{3} = 2\,{\text{mol}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{Fe}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 1\,{\text{mol}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{F}}{{\text{e}}_2}{\left( {{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}} \right)_3}$
Ratio of mol of ${\rm{Fe}}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}\,{\rm{and F}}{{\rm{e}}_2}{\left( {{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{4}}}} \right)_3} = \frac{2}{1} = 2$
subject: Chemistry

question: How many moles of iodine are liberated when 1 mole of potassium dichromate reacts with potassium iodide?
explanation:

question: The number of electrons involved in the reduction of one nitrate ion to hydrazine is
explanation:
Given, $\mathrm{\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}}$
The balanced reaction is
$\mathrm{2 \mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}+16 \mathrm{H}^{+}+14 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$
$\mathrm{\because}$ Number of electrons involved in the reduction of two nitrate ions $\mathrm{=14 \mathrm{e}^{-}}$
$\mathrm{\therefore}$ One nitrate ion will involve
$\mathrm{=\dfrac{14}{2} \mathrm{e}^{-}=7 \mathrm{e}^{-}}$
(OR)
Change in oxidation state of each nitrogen is
$\mathrm{7(+5}$ to -2$\mathrm{)}$. So, 7 electrons are involved.

no.of equivalent of $\mathrm{\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}=}$no.of equivalent of $\mathrm{\mathrm{FeC}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$

question:
Which of the following are disproportionation reactions?
(A) $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{NaOCl}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(B) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$
(C) $2 \mathrm{KMnO}_{4} \rightarrow \mathrm{~K}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{4}+\mathrm{MnO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$
(D) $3 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}+4 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow$
\begin{equation*}2 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+\mathrm{MnO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{equation*}
explanation:
(A) $\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^0 {{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}} + 2{\text{NaOH}} \to {\text{Na}}\mathop {{\text{Cl}}}\limits^{ - 1} + {\text{NaO}}\mathop {{\text{Cl}}}\limits^{ + 1} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}$
(B) ${{\text{H}}_2}\mathop {{{\text{O}}_2}}\limits^{ - 1} \to 2{{\text{H}}_2}\mathop {\text{O}}\limits^{ - 2} + \mathop {{{\text{O}}_2}}\limits^0 $
(C) $2\,{\text{K}}\mathop {{\text{Mn}}}\limits^{ + 7} {{\text{O}}_4} \to {{\text{K}}_2}\mathop {\text{M}}\limits^{ + \,6} {\text{n}}{{\text{O}}_4} + \mathop {\text{M}}\limits^{ + \,4} {\text{n}}{{\text{O}}_2} + {{\text{O}}_2}$
(D) $3\mathop {\text{M}}\limits^{ + \,6} {\text{nO}}_4^{2 - } + 4{{\text{H}}^ + } \to 2\mathop {\text{M}}\limits^{ + \,7} {\text{nO}}_4^ - + \mathop {\text{M}}\limits^{ + \,4} {\text{n}}{{\text{O}}_2} + 2{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{D}$ only are disproportionation reactions..
subject: Chemistry

question:
With reference to the redox properties of hydrogen peroxide $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}\right)$, which of these reactions are feasible?
(i) $2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(ii) $2 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+6 \mathrm{H}^{+}+5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow$
$2 \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+8 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+5 \mathrm{O}_{2}$
(iii) $2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}$
(iv) $2 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow$
$2{\text{Mn}}{{\text{O}}_2} + 2{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} + 3{{\text{O}}_2} + 2{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }$
explanation:
$\because \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ shows oxidising as well as reducing action.
Oxidising nature (i.e. acts as oxidising agent)
(i) In acidic solution,
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(ii) In alkaline solution,
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}$
Reducing nature (i.e. act as reducing agent)
$\underset{\text { (In acidic medium) }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{O}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
$\underset{\text { (In alkaline medium) }}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \rightarrow+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{O}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
Hence, (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv) all are possible and (3) is the correct answer.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Miscellaneous questions on types of redox reactions

question: Identify the oxidation states of Mn , when $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}$ ion undergoes disproportionation reaction under acidic medium.
explanation:
The disproportionation reaction for $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}$ occurs as follows :
$3\mathop {\text{M}}\limits^{ + \,6} {\text{nO}}_4^{2 - } + 4{{\text{H}}^ + } \to \mathop {\text{M}}\limits^{ + \,4} {\text{n}}{{\text{O}}_2} + 2\mathop {\text{M}}\limits^{ + \,7} {\text{nO}}_4^ - + 2{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}$
Hence, $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}$ is oxidised to $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$ and reduced to $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$. Thus, oxidation state of Mn when $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}$ undergoes disproportionation reaction under acidic medium are $ + 7$ and $ + 4$. Hence option (4) is the correct answer.
subject: Chemistry

question: Match the following
explanation:
$\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{Q} ; \mathrm{TiCl}_{4}(\mathrm{l})+2 \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{s}) \xrightarrow{\Delta}$ $\mathrm{Ti}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{MgCl}_{2}(\mathrm{~s})$
The above reaction is an example of metal displacement reaction in which more reactive metal. (i.e. Mg ) will displace less reactive metal (i.e. Ti) from its compound.
${\text{B}} - {\text{P}}\,;\,\,2{{\text{H}}_2}{{\text{O}}_2}({\text{aq}}) \to 2{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}\,(l) + {{\text{O}}_2}({\text{g}})$
The above reaction is an example of disproportionation reaction in which O -atom of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ is oxidised as $\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}\right)$ and also reduced to $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{S} ; \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
The above reaction is an example of combination reaction in which carbon(s) combines with $\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ to give $\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$.
${\text{D}} - {\text{R}}$ ; $2 \mathrm{NaH}(\mathrm{s}) \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ The above reaction is an example of decomposition reaction in which NaH decomposes to $\mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{s})$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$. So, option (1) is the correct answer.

question:
Chlorine undergoes disproportionation in alkaline medium as shown below
$\mathrm{aCl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{bOH}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{cClO}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}+\mathrm{dCl}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}+\mathrm{eH}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{l})}$
The values of ${\mathrm{a, b, c}}$ and ${\mathrm{d}}$ in a balanced redox reaction are respectively
explanation:
${\mathrm{\stackrel{(0)}{\mathrm{Cl}}_{2} \xrightarrow{(-2)} 2 \stackrel{(-1)}{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}}}$
$\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_2}}\limits^{(0)} \xrightarrow{{ + 2}}\mathop {2{\text{ClO}}}\limits^{( + 1)} $
So, the balanced redox reaction is
$\begin{aligned}2 \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-} & \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{ClO}^{-}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\& (\text { or }) \\\mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-} & \rightarrow \mathrm{ClO}^{-}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$
Thus, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{a}=1, \mathrm{~b}=2, \mathrm{c}=1}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{d}=1}}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Chemistry

question:
Which of the following reactions are disproportionation reactions?

(II) ${\mathrm{3 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}+4 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+\mathrm{MnO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$
(III) ${\mathrm{2 \mathrm{KMnO}_{4} \rightarrow \mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{4}+\mathrm{MnO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}}}$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
explanation:
A disproportionation reaction is a reaction in which, the same element of a particular species is simultaneously oxidised and reduced.
(I) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959128/preptracker/questions/q_18458_explanation_1764959128388.png (II) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959129/preptracker/questions/q_18458_explanation_1764959129047.png (III) ${\mathrm{\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}}}$gets only reduced as Mn is in highest oxidation state.
(IV) It is comproportionation reaction
So, the correct option is (4).

explanation:

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}+2 \times(-2)=0$

question: In the complex, $\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]^{+}$, the oxidation number of $\mathrm{Cr}$ is
explanation:
Let the oxidation number of $\mathrm{Cr}$ is $\mathrm{x}$ in the complex ${\left[ {{\rm{Cr}}{{\left( {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right]^{\rm{ + }}}$
$\therefore {\rm{x}} + 4 \times (0) + 2 \times ( - 1) = + 1$
${\rm{x}} - 2 = + 1$
${\rm{x}} = + 3$
Thus, oxidation number of $\mathrm{Cr}$ is +3 .
subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: When white $\mathrm{P}_{4}$ reacts with caustic soda, the products are $\mathrm{PH}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{NaH}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{2}$. This reaction is an example of disproportionation reaction.


Reason: In disproportionation reaction, the same substance may act simultaneously as an oxidising agent and as a reducing agent.
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953297/preptracker/questions/q_17759_explanation_1764953296770.png  Thus, phosphorus undergoes both oxidation as well as reduction simultaneously in the same reaction. So the option (1) is correct.

question:
When chlorine gas passes through a concentrated solution of alkali.
I. $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}_{2}}$ is oxidised to $\mathrm{\mathrm{ClO}^{-}}$
II. $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}_{2}}$ is reduced to $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}$
III. Products formed are $\mathrm{5 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{ClO}_{3}^{-}}$and $\mathrm{3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$.
Which of the above statement is/are correct?
explanation:
Statement II and III are correct, while the statement I is incorrect. Its correct form is as follows
When chlorine gas passes through a concentrated solution of alkali, the following chemical reaction occurs.
$\rm{\mathrm{Cl}_{2}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{Cl}^{-1}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{ClO}_{3}^{-}}$
Thus, $\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}_{2}}$ is reduced.
After balancing this equation, we have
$\rm{\underset{3 \mathrm{~mol}}{3 \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}+\underset{6 \mathrm{~mol}}{6 \mathrm{OH}^{-}}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow 5 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{ClO}_{3}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following elements does not show disproportionation tendency?
explanation: Being the most electronegative element, $\mathrm{\mathrm{F}}$ can only be reduced and hence it always shows an oxidation number of${\rm{ - 1}}$. Further, due to the absence of $\mathrm{d}$ - orbitals, it cannot be oxidised and hence it does not show +ve oxidation numbers. In other words, F cannot be simultaneously oxidised as well as reduced and hence does not show disproportionation reactions.
